data.title: Rockwell Automation: Structural Tailwinds, But With A Stretched Valuation
data.description:
Rockwell Automation, Inc. (NYSE:ROK), which described itself as the world's largest pure-play industrial automation and digital transformation company, has continued to deliver solid results despite a weak global economy. Higher interest rates and tariffs have resulted in cancellations or delays of CapEx projects. This has meant undershooting the total annual growth through the cycle revenue growth target of between 6% and 9%, shared during the company's investor day in November.

Rockwell has been facing headwinds for some time now. As we described in an article last year, the company was facing high inventory levels and increasing financing costs. Still, one key difference then was that the valuation was significantly more reasonable, and there were already signs that efficiency measures were helping improve profit margins. Since then, shares have outperformed the S&P 500 Index (SP500) by a wide margin.

After reviewing the most recent financial results, we see a number of positives and negatives. Revenue growth remains weak, which is not surprising given that CapEx spending growth remains well below the ten-year average, and expectations for the next six months are also quite underwhelming. On the positive side, efficiency measures are progressing well, with the company tracking ahead of target in terms of cost savings, profit margins remaining resilient, and some end markets performing well.

Fiscal Q3 2025 Results

For the third quarter, Rockwell reported achieving its roughly $250 million in productivity benefits one quarter earlier than expected, which helped deliver operating margins and adjusted earnings per share (EPS) above expectations. Sales increased close to 5% year-over-year, with total annual recurring revenue (ARR) up 7% year-over-year. Rockwell experienced the strongest growth in North America, which helped as the company generates almost two-thirds of its revenue from the region.

Still, there are reasons to temper optimism, as the book-to-bill ratio was close to 1.0, and part of the quarter's revenue growth outperformance was the result of some pull-forward from the fourth quarter.

End-market demand remains mixed, with the discrete segment performing well. This segment includes customers in the automotive, semiconductor, and warehouse automation industries. In particular, warehouse automation offers significant growth promise, with companies like UPS (UPS) investing to automate sorting and improve productivity and service quality. Rockwell has been betting heavily in this area, offering a number of warehouse automation services. Rockwell has also made some promising acquisitions, such as the 2023 purchase of Clearpath Robotics, a leader in autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) for industrial applications.

The hybrid segment also performed relatively well, catering mostly to customers in the food and beverage and life sciences industries. Where the company saw significant weakness was in the process segment, where customers are exposed to volatile commodity prices, and some sectors are experiencing overcapacity.

Despite the relatively weak macroeconomic environment, Rockwell managed to deliver solid earnings, with diluted EPS of $2.60 and adjusted EPS of $2.82. An increase in compensation expense was mostly offset by cost reduction measures, while the benefit of higher sales was partially offset by currency and other headwinds.

Financials

Rockwell maintains a very solid balance sheet, with an "A" credit rating, approximately $400 million in cash and short-term investments, and leverage of roughly 2x adjusted debt to EBITDA. This gives the company optionality to take advantage of potential acquisitions, with the company having previously said that they are willing to allow leverage to increase to roughly 3.5x adjusted debt to EBITDA for a strategic acquisition.

As Rockwell cannot influence the macroeconomic environment, it will be difficult for the company to increase earnings by growing sales. Instead, it has focused on profit margins, taking several proactive measures. These include increasing prices, in part to offset tariff costs, as well as self-help measures to improve efficiency. Cost reductions are being implemented in various ways, including supplier consolidation and renegotiations, supply chain optimization measures, and portfolio optimization, including the reduction of low-volume and low-margin SKUs. Additionally, Rockwell is preparing to invest roughly $2 billion over the next five years in modernizing its plants, digital infrastructure, and talent. As well, it is integrating artificial intelligence (AI) into its business processes and supply chain.

Peer Comparison

Compared with peers, Rockwell is considered more of a "pure-play" on industrial automation relative to peers such as Emerson (EMR) and Honeywell (HON), and also more focused on North America compared to European peers like Schneider Electric (OTCPK:SBGSF) and ABB (OTCPK:ABBNY). Still, some of its competitors are very large firms that can outspend it in R&D, and some appear to be ahead in terms of software integration, such as Schneider Electric after it acquired AVEVA. Over the past five years, Rockwell has outpaced its U.S. peers but trailed Schneider and ABB.

However, Emerson and Honeywell still have significantly higher profit margins, with Rockwell only showing a higher net income margin than Schneider Electric in the list of peers shown in the table below.

Despite not having delivered the highest growth over the past few years nor having the highest profit margins, Rockwell Automation appears to be the most expensive company in the peer group below, with both its trailing-twelve-months and forward Price/Earnings ratios meaningfully above those of its peers.

Shareholder Returns

Rockwell should be given credit for a generous shareholder return policy, where most of the free cash flow not needed for acquisitions or debt reduction is returned as either dividends or share repurchases. Just in the past quarter, Rockwell repurchased about half a million shares for roughly $123 million, with close to $1 billion remaining under its authorization. Still, given the current stretched valuation, the dividend yields only about 1.5%, and even the shareholder yield, which includes repurchases, is below 3%.

Future Outlook

During the most recent earnings call, management was asked about the mix of pull-forward versus project delays and weakness in several end markets. Rockwell CEO Blake D. Moret described the pull-forwards as largely product-related, while delays were project-centric. He also shared that Automotive and Home & Personal Care were solid end markets, but the tone suggested very cautious optimism.

[...] So the potential pull forwards that we highlighted are really on the product side. So if people are going to make a pull-forward purchase, they're going to do that in the product area. And we thought that, while, again, we have good processes to avoid taking orders that are obviously opportunistic, let's say, to avoid a price increase, we think it's prudent to give allowance for the possibility that we did see some pull forward in our product orders. The delays are more on the project side, and you see that primarily in the configure-to-order portion of intelligent devices, as well as in life cycle services. So that would be the distribution.

Now, as you go through the different verticals. Automotive had a good Q3. We returned to good year-over-year growth. Again, I would look at that as a little bit of that prudence that we don't want to get too far ahead of ourselves, and say this is the beginning of a trend. But there were definitely some nice wins within Automotive. There's a huge installed base that has to be serviced to be able to continue to keep production running at a good rate. The other vertical that isn't shown in the exhibit is Home & Personal Care, which had strong growth year-over-year in the quarter. So you look at that, and I'd say it's a largely balanced view.

Decades of Allen-Bradley hardware on plant floors mean Rockwell is deeply embedded in customers' automation architecture. Replacing these systems is costly, risky, and generally avoided unless there is a very compelling reason. These customer switching costs give Rockwell strong pricing power.

Now with an increased focus on complete solutions and integrating both hardware and software, switching costs are further increasing and are helping provide recurring revenue and widening Rockwell's competitive moat. Advanced software integration is resulting in what the company calls "software-defined automation," which allows for things like AI-enabled maintenance, digital twins, and cloud-native manufacturing systems.

Rockwell's competitive moat is further strengthened by a strong partner ecosystem, which includes companies like Ansys, Amazon's AWS (AMZN), Cisco (CSCO), and PTC (PTC), among others. Rockwell also has a number of structural tailwinds in its favor, including an aging global workforce, a need for manufacturing productivity improvements to justify near-shoring, and more manufacturing flexibility to adapt production if consumer needs change or in case of supply-chain disruptions.

In the near term, guidance is for fiscal year 2025 to deliver adjusted EPS of $10 at the midpoint, with free cash flow conversion of close to 100%, with sales growth expected in the range of -2% to +1%. This profit would be achievable in part thanks to the cost reduction and margin expansion initiatives the company has implemented.

Valuation

Rockwell trades at a P/E GAAP (TTM) of close to 41x and a P/E Non-GAAP (FWD) of roughly 35x. Rockwell deserves a premium versus old-school industrial companies (XLI) because of the tailwinds benefiting industrial automation, its software that generates attractive recurring revenue, and a large installed base. However, the elevated Price/Earnings multiples set a very high bar. Any disappointment, whether from the economy or the company, could result in valuation multiples being rerated lower.

Risks

The main risk we see for Rockwell Automation investors is the stretched valuation that leaves little room for disappointment, either from operational mistakes by the company itself or simply a deteriorating macroeconomic environment.

Currently, price and cost remain relatively favorable, even with tariffs, thanks to resilient pricing power and disciplined cost takeout. Still, management believes some third-quarter shipments and orders were pulled forward from the fourth quarter due to trade uncertainty. The end-market trend is also concerning, as one analyst during the earnings call pointed out; in one of their earnings slides, they downgraded outlooks for six out of nine of their markets compared to the same evaluation in April. CapEx remains extremely weak, even in North America, which is supposed to benefit from near-shoring.

These risks are mitigated by cost reductions and efficiency measures, which have already delivered $250 million of savings in fiscal 2025 within three quarters. The company's efforts to modernize and automate its own manufacturing also mitigate higher cost headwinds and create a path toward higher profit margins without necessarily requiring a surge in global manufacturing CapEx spending.

Conclusion

Rockwell has delivered solid results despite continued headwinds, including elevated interest rates and tariffs. The company has shown its pricing power by passing tariff-related cost increases to customers, and there are signs that the increased integration of software and more comprehensive solutions are widening its competitive moat while increasing recurring revenue. It will also be interesting to see the results of its investments to optimize and automate its own manufacturing. For these reasons, we remain optimistic about the company's long-term prospects. That said, the current valuation is quite high, and any disappointment could result in a very significant share price decline.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Hot CPI And A Cold PPI: Are U.S. Equities About To Collapse?
data.description:
I remain bullish on the broader markets as the consensus is now pricing in 3 rate cuts by the end of the year. I believe this will stimulate US equities in Q4 as more liquidity flows into the markets.

In my previous articles, I noted that I was content with just 2 cuts (and I was actually hesitant on a second cut due to inflation data ticking up as I noticed tariffs mostly passed through to consumers). After revising the latest data, it seems that the consensus is pricing in 3 rate cuts by the December 10 FOMC meeting. I believe the key factors include

- Unemployment is ticking up, especially after the BLS’s annual early estimate, indicating 911,000 fewer payroll jobs than previously reported through March 2025.

- The latest PPI and CPI data.

In this article, I focus on the latter, as last week I covered unemployment.

A Cold PPI and A Hot CPI

On September 10, the U.S. Producer Price Index for August was released by the BLS. The top line for final demand showed a -10 bps contraction m/m, after two consecutive expansions in July and June (+70 bps and +10 bps, respectively). This was significantly below the consensus of +30 bps.

I see the top-line reading of the August PPI as a double-edged sword. On the positive side, it is an additional reason for Chair Powell to cut rates more than once this year, as subtly hinted by Trump earlier this week:

Just out: No Inflation!!! “Too Late” must lower the RATE, BIG, right now...

So far, the consensus for a rate cut in September is 92.7%. However, looking at the consensus for the December meeting, there is only a 1% chance of just a single rate cut this year. As seen below, the consensus seems to be toward 3 rate cuts.

On the negative side, the soft CPI read sparked some fears among market participants, with some sources already pointing to softening demand due to tariffs among the drivers. So, the big question is: are tariffs leading to a slowdown in consumer spending?

The data says no. I'll look first at the PPI and then at the CPI.

The table below shows the different parts of the PPI that contributed to the top-line -0.1% contraction.

As seen above, the major factor was a -1.7% m/m contraction in trade services. Goods actually saw a 0.1% m/m expansion, with foods experiencing the same increase. Here is the BLS explanation for the increase in the final demand goods index:

A major factor in the August increase in the index for final demand goods was a 2.3-percent advance in prices for tobacco products. The indexes for beef and veal; processed poultry; printed circuit assemblies, boards, modules and modems; and electric power also rose. In contrast, prices for utility natural gas decreased 1.8 percent. The indexes for fresh and dry vegetables, chicken eggs, and copper base scrap also fell.

Looking at the -0.2% m/m contraction in services, as I noted above, the biggest factor was a -1.7% m/m margin compression in trade services. Therefore, it seems to me that tariffs are being absorbed by enterprises and not passed through to consumers. The biggest drag seems to come from machinery & vehicle wholesaling margins, decreasing by -3.9% m/m:

Three quarters of the August decrease in prices for final demand services can be attributed to a 3.9-percent decline in margins for machinery and vehicle wholesaling. The indexes for professional and commercial equipment wholesaling, chemicals and allied products wholesaling, furniture retailing, food and alcohol retailing, and data processing and related services also moved lower. In contrast, prices for portfolio management advanced 2.0 percent. The indexes for truck transportation of freight and for apparel wholesaling also increased.

To me, that decrease in machinery & vehicle wholesaling margins is a strong sign that vehicle wholesalers are biting the bullet by absorbing the tariffs.

Moving on now to the CPI, the hot reading in August confirms that consumer spending remains strong. The CPI-U (for all urban consumers) increased by 0.4% m/m and 2.9% yoy, quite above Powell's 2% historical target and the consensus of 0.3% m/m.

Zooming out and looking at inflation over the last 10 years, the 2.9% yoy in August is not a massive increase, although it is the highest reading since January this year.

The main factors that moved the CPI up were used and new cars (1%), food (0.5%), apparel (0.5%), and energy (0.7%).

Therefore, I don't fully agree with Trump on his statement that there is no inflation simply because the PPI was down. As I've shown above, the decline was mainly driven by lower trade services margins. The decline in these margins is not an indication that retail prices are going down; it's an indication that producer margins are declining, possibly due to biting the bullet on tariffs.

On the other hand, the CPI reading, which measures prices paid by consumers at retail, was hot. If prices go up, regardless of whether producer margins are down (i.e., a decline in PPI), inflation goes up. So, what's my point?

Conclusion

Don't pop a bottle of champagne for the economic results released this week. Inflation is still high, as noted by a hot CPI read. Consumers are still spending despite the hike in prices, and the Fed has fewer reasons to cut rates. I see the PPI decline as an indication that US importers are absorbing margins, which is a negative factor from an earnings perspective.

Bottom line: monitor Chair Powell's remarks next Wednesday to see any hints at more than a rate cut this year.

data.title: Lucid Group: Drive The Gravity, Avoid The Stock
data.description:
Lucid Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:LCID), arguably the maker of the most aesthetically appealing EVs in the United States, launched its all-new Gravity SUV in Europe earlier this week at the IAA Mobility summit in Munich, Germany. This marks the beginning of Lucid taking the fight directly to European luxury electric SUV manufacturers such as Mercedes-Benz, BMW, and Audi. Similar to how I was truly impressed by the quality of the Lucid Air last year when I visited the company’s Dubai showroom, I expect potential EV buyers in Europe to find the Gravity a true technological marvel. Despite acknowledging product superiority in August 2024, I took a bearish stance on LCID stock as I found significant barriers to scalability. Last April, I reiterated my bearish stance on Lucid stock despite acknowledging the possibility of Lucid gaining market share at the expense of Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). Lucid Gravity, in my opinion, validates the engineering prowess of Lucid, but unfortunately, the company remains uninvestable as there is a widening gap between the appeal of Lucid as an automaker and Lucid as an investment.

Lucid Gravity Deserves The Hype

Lucid Gravity is truly a remarkable product.

From a performance and powertrain perspective, Lucid Gravity has exhibited supercar-level acceleration capabilities. In a MotorTrend drag race, the Gravity Dream Edition with dual motors completed a quarter mile in just 10.5 seconds, matching a Lamborghini Huracan. The Gravity proved faster than some of the most popular supercar models, including the Lamborghini Urus and Porsche Cayenne Turbo GT. When it comes to the powertrain, Gravity shares the same technology as Lucid Air Sapphire, which, according to Car and Driver, is the quickest car they have ever tested, as it achieved a 0-60 mph time of just 1.9 seconds.

Lucid also scores big on the software side, with the company recently rolling out a software update to the ADAS, enabling hands-free drive assist and hands-free lane change assist. According to company officials, these features would be available on Lucid Gravity by the end of the year. Some of these features will be exclusively available to DreamDrive Pro subscribers. Lucid’s partnership with ridesharing giants such as Uber Technologies (UBER) validates its software architecture, as Uber is focused on partnering with automakers that offer seamless integration to Level 4 autonomous systems.

Lucid Gravity also scored a major win when it became the first non-Tesla vehicle to offer a built-in NACS connector, which allows native access to the Tesla Supercharger network. Range anxiety, arguably, is among the primary reasons that prevent Americans from embracing EVs, and Lucid seems to be focused on addressing this head-on.

On top of all these things, Lucid Gravity certainly looks very sleek for an SUV, in my opinion.

Exhibit 1: Lucid Gravity

Overall, from a performance, software, technology, and design perspective, I believe Lucid Gravity deserves the hype in the market.

Lucid Remains An Investment Minefield

Lucid has a great product. As discussed above, the company has a superior product that attracts premium prices. That said, as investors, I believe we should master the skill of differentiating companies that sell great products and companies that are great investments. Lucid, unfortunately, is an uninvestable business that sells a great product, in my opinion.

There are three main reasons behind my cautious stance on Lucid.

  1. The lackluster financial position of the company.
  2. The scaling problem faced by the company.
  3. Competition.

I will now address these concerns briefly.

For Q2, Lucid reported revenue of $259.4 million. This was a record quarterly high for the company. However, a quick look at the costs reveals Lucid is a long way away from turning gross profits. In Q2, the cost of revenue came to $531.8 million, more than double the reported revenue for the same period. To put this simply, it costs the company more than double its revenue just to produce and sell the vehicles. For an automaker, especially an EV maker vying for market share in an already crowded field, this is not the ideal situation to be in. Needless to say, the company is bleeding cash thick and fast. In Q2, Lucid reported a free cash outflow of ~$1 billion. Lucid ended the quarter with ~$2.8 billion in cash and short-term investments, which seems sufficient to ensure it operates as a going concern in the foreseeable future. However, this does not mean that investors should not be concerned about the sustainability of this liquidity runway.

Exhibit 2: Lucid’s free cash flow

Moving to the scalability challenges faced by Lucid, it seems reasonable to conclude that this is an even bigger problem than liquidity, for now. Based on the updated guidance, Lucid now expects to produce between 18,000 and 20,000 vehicles this year. Previously, the company had guided for a production volume of 20,000 vehicles.

Exhibit 3: Lucid’s 2025 outlook

So far, in the first half of the year, Lucid has produced 6,075 vehicles. At this pace, Lucid is very likely to fall short of meeting the updated, lowered production guidance for the full year. What is even more concerning is that Lucid, even at very low levels of production, is still building inventory. For context, in Q2, the company produced 3,863 vehicles but delivered only 3,309 vehicles. Although deliveries grew almost 40% YoY in Q2, the bigger picture confirms that Lucid’s production is still outpacing deliveries. Right on cue, total inventory value reported by Lucid has swollen from $407 million at the end of 2024 to $713 million as of Q2. Lucid, based on these observations, is facing a demand problem as well.

More scalability problems can be seen when you carefully go through the 10-Q filing. The company states that it is dependent on single-source suppliers, which creates a massive risk of business failure, as just a single point of failure would deal a hefty blow to production efficiency. Some other barriers to expanding its scale include the lack of a notable dealership network, massive costs incurred while expanding internationally, and market entry challenges in countries with a well-established EV ecosystem.

The competitive environment does not look promising as well. Large-scale automakers are aggressively pursuing an EV reality, and a few high-profile electric SUVs are expected to hit the market soon. Some of these highly expected electric SUV launches include Audi TT EV (2027), Bentley EV SUV (2027), BMW iX5 (2027), BMW iX3 (2026), Buick Electra E5 (2026), and a Ford mid-size electric truck as well.

On top of all this, I believe recent catalysts do not represent a major turnaround in prospects for Lucid. While securing partnerships with mobility giants such as Uber is a positive turn of events, the absolute numbers are not substantial enough to make a major impact on Lucid’s financials in the foreseeable future. As part of the deal between Lucid and Uber/Nuro, the company is planning to deploy around 20,000 vehicles spread over six years. Given that my main concerns revolve around Lucid’s inability to sell vehicles profitably, this long-term deal does little to change my stance on Lucid stock.

Takeaway

The more I look at Lucid vehicles, the more I fall for the brand. Lucid Gravity, the electric SUV, is a timely follow-up to Lucid Air, with performance specs that rival even the best names in the industry. While there is a lot to like about Lucid vehicles, unfortunately, the company remains uninvestable due to several reasons, including scalability challenges, weak demand, and intensifying competition. I believe investing in LCID stock today is nothing more than a bet on an unforeseen catalyst dramatically changing the fortunes of the company.

data.title: Fed Funds Rate Cuts Won't Necessarily Lower Residential Mortgage Rates
data.description:
Recently, there has been considerable discussion and stock market bets on Fed Funds rate cuts driving mortgage rates down. If that happens, it will significantly benefit the struggling housing industry, REITs, and Corporate America in general. The markets currently believe there is an 88% chance of a 0.25% Fed Funds rate cut next week.

While a Fed Funds rate cut next week appears likely, significantly lower mortgage rates are much less certain. This is because Fed Funds are short-term rates. They can change daily. They actually have less impact on businesses and consumers than fixed rates. Most residential mortgages, car loans, and corporate debt, outside of lines of credit, are fixed-rate. Residential mortgages are generally 30-year fixed-rate loans, the longest terms out there. They act closest to a 10-year Treasury since their average life is close to that. For that reason, they are often benchmarked to a 10-year treasury. That makes them less affected by short-term rates than any other type of loan. The loans most impacted by Fed Funds rate cuts are lines of credit to businesses, home equity lines, and credit cards.

The Fed has had little impact on longer-term rates. Their primary tool there is something called quantitative easing. That's simply where they go out and buy a whole bunch of longer-term treasuries and residential mortgage securities in an attempt to lower long-term rates. This action has been ineffective in the past. I actually wrote an article about it in January 2016, showing no impact in the 2007-2009 time frame.

Historical Results

The best way to illustrate my point is to look at the last three periods of Fed Funds cuts. On April 1, 2007, as the deep recession of 2007-2009 got going, Fed Funds were at 5.26%, and the 30-year residential mortgage averaged 6.70%. By December 1, 2008, Fed Funds had dropped by 5.10% to 0.16%. The 30-year mortgage had dropped only 1.41% to 5.29%. These are illustrated in the chart below.

The next period of declining Fed Funds rates was between July 1, 2019, and April 1, 2020. During that time, Fed Funds dropped by 2.35%, while the 30-year mortgage dropped only 0.46%. Mortgage rates did continue to drop after Fed Funds hit close to zero, but the eventual difference was still wide.

Most recently, Fed funds dropped by 1.00% between August 1, 2024, and the end of 2024. The 30-year mortgage rate actually increased during that time by 0.46%. It has since given up that increase but still remains around where it was in August 2024, when Fed Funds rates started getting cut.

I did the math, averaging the last 3 Fed Funds rate cut periods that are shown above. The 30-year residential mortgage rate, on average, dropped by only 17% of the amount that Fed Funds did while Fed Funds were being cut. In the last period, the 30-year residential mortgage rate actually went up.

One more chart is added below to show the time period of Fed Fund rate cuts and their impact on inflation. Increasing Fed Fund rates generally reduces inflation, while reducing them generally increases inflation. There is a lag as to when the impact is fully felt.

Why the Disconnect

Here is why simply lowering Fed Funds rates is generally not going to result in a large decline in residential mortgage rates. There are a lot more factors that go into longer-term rates than Fed Funds rates. First of all, Fed Funds rates are 100% manipulated by the Fed. It is an artificial rate. Longer-term rates are market-based. Fed funds are just one of many factors that affect it. These factors are listed below.

Factors pushing long-term rates up include;

Large fiscal deficits like what we have now. The pricing of anything is a function of supply and demand, and deficits create more supply. That increases yields.

  • Foreigners buying fewer treasuries pushes rates up. A lot of foreigners and foreign governments see the U.S. as a less friendly place right now.
  • Increased inflation pushes long-term rates higher. Investors demand a higher return to offset higher inflation. Tariffs are expected by economists to increase inflation.
  • A strong economy pushes rates higher as the demand for loans increases. Demand from expanding businesses exceeds supply. The economy is weakening right now, so this one isn’t in play.
  • Increased housing demand increases long-term rates. More demand for anything usually increases prices, including for money.
  • Quantitative tightening by the Fed pushes rates up. This is where the Fed sells off, or allows to mature, its portfolio of treasuries and fixed-rate mortgages. This has been happening for 3 years now but may soon reverse.

Factors pushing long-term rates down are the opposites of those listed above.

Another major factor that pushes down long-term rates is a flight to safety during a recession. This increases demand for U.S. treasuries, increasing the price and lowering the rate. If we go into a recession, this will come into play.

There is one more factor to keep in mind, which is the term premium. Longer-term loans usually have a higher yield than shorter-term loans. This is due to the extra yield investors want in order to hold a security long term. Longer-term rates change less on a daily or monthly basis than short-term rates.

Based on the above, Fed Funds are just one of many factors impacting residential mortgage rates. Historically, it has had an impact, though a muted one.

How to Play It

A lot of investors are betting on Fed Funds rate cuts also lowering fixed-rate residential mortgage rates. They are buying home builders, REITs, and corporations with a lot of fixed-rate debt. A moderately sized bet here may make sense, but don’t bet the farm. Historically, this hasn’t happened in a large enough way. Also, with tariffs likely to increase inflation, it is possible there are little or no Fed Fund rate cuts. If inflation gets out of control, Fed Funds may even go up.

If you own long-term bonds or ETFs of long-term bonds, my recommendation is to hold or possibly accumulate a little more. The latter is a defensive or hedging move based on the weakening economy. Bonds, especially treasuries, are generally a haven when the stock market struggles. If you own an ETF like the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:TLT), I would recommend buying a little more if you have a little and holding if it is already a significant position.

Takeaway

If the economy continues to weaken, there will likely be some movement down in residential mortgage rates. It's certainly possible that Fed Funds rate cuts will lower residential mortgage rates enough to create a resurgence in housing. But based on historical results and all the factors that go into longer-term rates, that shouldn’t be your base case.

data.title: The U.S. Labor Market Isn't What It Seems: Cracks Are Widening
data.description:
Something is changing

The much-awaited recession never materialized, and the U.S. economy has remained resilient even though there was a lot of talk about a lost decade. As usual, the doom and gloom predictions were wrong, and the best option was simply to stay invested.

In particular, the labor market has been the pride of the U.S. economy, which remains strong despite interest rates being kept at decade-high levels.

The unemployment rate is currently higher than in July 2023 (3.5%), but 4.3% is still relatively good compared with historical levels. We can state that the U.S. economy is in good shape overall, but what if these figures are not 100% accurate?

Up until now, analysts had taken the data’s accuracy for granted; however, after repeated downward revisions from the Bureau of Labor Statistics [BLS], new doubts are arising. To be clear, this doesn’t mean that data have been manipulated; BLS revisions are routine. I am not implying any malicious intent, just an internal problem about how data have been calculated.

Last month, the BLS revised downward May and June non-farm payrolls by more than 250,000 units; this significant change brought the data to levels experienced during the pandemic. Summer hires have been lower than forecasts, but it gets worse.

Few days ago there was another revision for the month of June: from +14,000 to -13,000. Basically, after 5 years, the U.S. economy started losing jobs, and the first cracks emerged in the flagship of the country. High employment and a dynamic labor market were two factors that contributed to avoiding “the most predicted recession ever”, now I am less optimistic compared to just a couple of months ago. July data were revised upward by 6,000 units; however, they can’t offset the weakness of May and June.

Graphically, we can see an evident drop in new hirings, and we can't know if the negative surprises are over.

President Trump didn’t appreciate BLS revisions and fired the former commissioner Erika McEntarfer with the accusation of manipulating data in order to discredit him and Republicans. As of today, there is no tangible proof to bear his action, and Erika McEntarfer will probably be replaced by E.J. Antoni, an economist with a point of view closer to Trump’s. I watched some of his interviews, and I remained quite perplexed about how extreme he is on some crucial topics like the importance of central banks.

A lot of people ask, well, what would you replace the Fed with? Why do you need to replace it with anything? When you remove a tumor from a patient, with what do you replace it? Nothing. You’ve restored them to health. I mean, there’s no reason why we can’t go back to a commodity-backed currency like we had before the Federal Reserve.

In other words, the Fed is just a burden, and the U.S. economy can easily do without it. I, personally, find this view too drastic and wrong (to say the least), but that’s just my opinion. Some relevant economists, like Jason Furman, didn’t appreciate his appointment as well:

E.J. Antoni is completely unqualified to be BLS Commissioner. He is an extreme partisan and does not have any relevant expertise. He would be a break from decades of nonpartisan technocrats.

Jason Furman

Anyway, the point is not to put Antoni in a bad light; he might be the best commissioner BLS has ever had, and I hope so. However, the way he may become BLS commissioner (it is not yet official, pending Senate approval) is what worries me the most. Erika McEntarfer was seemingly fired without any evidence against her just because of a downward revision: that’s the real problem, in my opinion.

This event might create a significant precedent that won’t favor future data accuracy. If a downward revision means losing the job, I believe the next BLS commissioner will definitely take this into consideration.

The incentive to not revise data for the worst can lead to meaningful differences between what data says and what is really happening in the economy. At first you might not trust the unemployment rate, then the inflation rate. In any case, an incorrect inflation rate doesn’t prevent an increasingly empty shopping cart. It just hides the financial stress faced by the lower-middle class.

Finally, BLS also revised the data for the year that ends in March 2025: 911,000 fewer jobs added than previously expected. That means that the first cracks in the job market began at the end of the previous administration; nonetheless, only today are we aware of them.

Why labor market is deteriorating

Even though still strong, the labor market is deteriorating. Why is it so? In my opinion, there are three main reasons.

The first is that AI is starting to have some impact on the labor market. On one hand, this amazing technology is changing the world for the better, on the other hand represents an incentive to hire less. If a machine does the job better than a human and it is cheaper, what stops a firm from preferring the former? Clearly, this doesn’t apply to all jobs, but inevitably some of them will be replaced. According to recent reports, AI is already replacing thousands of jobs each month.

The second reason lies in the tightening of immigration policies since President Trump took office. From January to July, 1.20 million immigrants left the U.S. workforce. This figure includes not only workers that were illegally in the country but also those who got all their papers in order. According to Pia Orrenius, Labor economist at the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas, this problem shouldn’t be underestimated since 50% of employment growth depends on immigrants:

The influx across the border from what we can tell is essentially stopped, and that's where we were getting millions and millions of migrants over the last four years. That has had a huge impact on the ability to create jobs.

The third and last reason that deteriorated the labor market is tariffs, a factor that builds constant worries in companies. Tariffs create uncertainty about future growth, and this doesn’t favor new hirings. That’s why companies that belong to sectors where tariffs have great relevance are firing the most.

The tariff impact on hiring is now undeniable. The manufacturing renaissance, the hiring boom, is just not happening.

Joe Brusuelas, chief economist at RSM

Paradoxically, tariffs are experiencing the opposite effect of what President Trump predicted. For the fourth month in a row, manufacturing employment declined, and there are 78,000 fewer jobs compared to last year.

Recession or not?

A rising unemployment is typically a recessionary signal, yet in the last years we got used to false alarms from indicators that once proved flawless. One of them is Sahm Rule.

In August 2024, the indicator overcame the 0.50% recession threshold (0.57%), and that was a big deal: over the previous 40 years, it always anticipated a recession. In the end, as we all know, the indicator ended up being unreliable, but a doubt arises: what if it failed to predict a recession due to incorrect data from the BLS?

Another aspect that fuels my doubts about U.S. labor market resilience concerns unemployment by race. The 4.30% figure includes all workers over the age of 16, but digging deep, we can notice some interesting information.

The white unemployment rate has gone from 3.80% of the last year to the current 3.70%; Asian unemployment got an improvement as well, from 4.10% to 3.60%. However, Black or African American unemployment rose to 7.50% (the highest since October 2021), compared to 6.10% in August 2024. We are talking about a marked worsening that highlights a totally different trend compared to White and Asian unemployment: this doesn’t bode well for the U.S. economy.

Black workers have been associated with the saying “last hired, first fired,” and some economists believe that through their unemployment trend, you can understand which phase the economy is in. In other words, when they get laid off at greater rates than other classes, it could signal that a recession might be close since they are more involved in jobs sensitive to the business cycle. That’s why a rising Black unemployment rate might imply deteriorating economic activity and eventually a potential recession.

Conclusion

Downward revisions, dismissal without cause, and rising Black unemployment are all signs that something is changing in the U.S. labor market. In comparison with last year, I think a deterioration is evident, and since the factors that triggered it are already there (AI, lower immigration, tariffs), I am skeptical about an improvement over the next months.

Of course, this doesn’t mean that a recession is close and we should sell everything. Even if the unemployment rate reached 5%, it wouldn’t be the end of the world; the problem emerges when the upward trend is out of control. We definitely are not there yet, even though we can’t exclude this scenario in the future.

The purpose of this article is not to spread panic but to provide food for thought about a crucial matter for the U.S. economy. We tend to focus just on the bottom line, 4.30%, but there is much more behind that simple figure.

data.title: Elastic: Rebound Isn't As Strong As It Appears
data.description:
Elastic's (NYSE:ESTC) Q1 results significantly surprised to the upside, with revenue growth reaccelerating and margins continuing to improve. First quarter strength was reportedly broad based, although Elastic's results probably weren't as strong as the headline numbers suggest. The results of companies like Snowflake (SNOW), MongoDB (MDB) and Elastic could be taken as an early sign that AI spend is beginning to work its way through the software ecosystem. Search is still a minority of Elastic's business though, and AI is a relatively small part of Search.

I previously suggested that while Elastic's stock was relatively inexpensive, there were more attractive opportunities available elsewhere. This has largely proven to be the case, with stocks like MongoDB offering far stronger returns. My opinion hasn't really changed since then either. Elastic is a solid company, and its valuation is still reasonable, but I don't see it generating compelling returns for shareholders.

Market Conditions

Elastic suggested that demand generally strengthened in the first quarter, and there was a stabilization in the US public sector relative to several months earlier. MongoDB and Snowflake also both reported strong results in the quarter, pointing towards a general improvement in conditions for data infrastructure companies. Strength isn’t universal though, with C3.ai (AI) recording extremely poor results.

I question just how strong the demand environment is at the moment though, as Elastic benefited from a number of temporary factors in the quarter. The number of Elastic job openings and the number of job openings mentioning elasticsearch in the job requirements also don't suggest a dramatic shift in conditions.

In addition to stronger demand, Elastic may be benefitting from platformization. For example, Pinecone is reportedly considering the sale of its business, driven by rising competition and the loss of Notion as a customer. Notion had reportedly been spending $4-$5 million annually. Pinecone is perhaps the most high profile of the vector database startups. The company raised capital at a $750 million valuation in 2023, but it appears to be struggling in the face of peers with more comprehensive offerings. Pinecone’s struggles tie in with Elastic’s commentary regarding increased customer interest in platformization. Oracle, IBM, MongoDB and Snowflake have all been proposed as potential suitors, but MongoDB already has its own vector database solution which is integrated into its platform.

Elastic Business Updates

Gen AI was a demand driver in Q1, with customers using Elastic's platform for building conversational AI and agentic applications. More than 2,200 Elastic Cloud customers are now using Elastic for gen AI use cases, and over 330 of these customers are spending in excess of $100,000 annually. The first quarter also saw Elastic add more $1 million ACV Elastic Cloud gen AI customers than the previous 2 quarters combined. While AI is contributing to Elastic’s business, the contribution is still small at the moment. This is not surprising given that migrations take time, and that most companies are still experimenting with AI.

Platform consolidation was reportedly a tailwind in the quarter, with AI helping Elastic to gain share in observability and security. Elastic’s Security business was an area of particular strength in Q1. Of note, around a third of new and expansion security wins in Q1 involved competitive displacements. This aligns with prior commentary around the legacy SIEM replacement opportunity. Something that has also been highlighted by the likes of CrowdStrike (CRWD). Elastic Security unifies SIEM and XDR into a single AI-powered platform, although I tend to think that Elastic's architecture is inferior to the likes of CrowdStrike and SentinelOne (S).

Financial Analysis

Elastic generated $415 million revenue in Q1, an increase of 20% YoY, with growth solid across both cloud and self-managed environments. On a constant currency basis, growth was 18%, compared to 16% in the prior quarter. CRPO also increased 18% YoY to $956 million.

Elastic Cloud revenue growth was 24% in Q1, compared to 23% in the prior quarter. Elastic also reported its strongest non-Elastic Cloud quarter since 2022, meaning ASC606 accounting was likely an important contributor to strength in the quarter.

Elastic expects $415-417 million in revenue in the second quarter, implying 14% YoY growth at the midpoint. For the full fiscal year, revenue is expected to be $1.679-1.689 billion, also up 14% YoY. Improved visibility and expectations of a stronger macro environment have reportedly been baked into updated guidance, although these figures appear to be overly conservative.

Elastic had 21,550 customers at the end of Q1, with growth of the total customer base remaining tepid. This should be expected given that Elastic increased its focus on enterprise accounts and high-potential mid-market customers around a year ago. The number of Elastic customers with more than $100,000 ACV increased 13% YoY to 1,550 in the first quarter.

Elastic has also suggested that its net expansion rate will strengthen in coming quarters. While Elastic increased prices in Q1, growth was driven more by consumption than pricing.

Elastic's Subscription gross margin rebounded in the first quarter, which may have been supported by increased pricing. Elastic also recognized a onetime credit of roughly $4 million, which was related to cloud infrastructure costs. The company's Services gross margin remains under pressure though, which is suggestive of ongoing demand weakness.

Elastic's operating margin and free cash flows continue to improve on the back of operating leverage and cost discipline. The company's non-GAAP operating margin was 16% in Q1, and it is near breakeven on a GAAP basis.

Conclusion

Elastic's revenue growth rate rebounded in the first quarter, helping to drive the stock higher. The reason for this isn’t really clear, though. While consumption appears to be improving, net customer additions remain soft. Elastic also benefitted from a somewhat easier comparable period and foreign exchange tailwinds. In addition, the timing of Easter and price increases likely provided a small boost. As did the accounting treatment of non-cloud revenue. As a result, I am sceptical that Elastic's business has reached a turning point.

Despite its share price moving higher in recent weeks, Elastic's revenue multiple is still fairly modest given its current financial performance, particularly now that growth has stabilized and Elastic is approaching GAAP breakeven. I continue to believe that Elastic needs a stronger narrative before the stock moves meaningfully higher though. Stronger AI related demand would be the most obvious driver of this, although I am not overly optimistic on this front.

data.title: Testing Gold And Bitcoin As Portfolio Diversification Tools
data.description:
By James Picerno

Alternative assets are enjoying a banner year. Two of the hottest: bitcoin and gold. Both are outperforming stocks, bonds, broad measures of commodities and property shares via real estate investment trusts by wide margins so far in 2025.

Holding bitcoin, gold, or both has been a win-win for portfolio design lately, which suggests that these assets should be included in every asset allocation plan. But a closer look at the numbers reminds that there’s more to evaluating assets than simply picking the winners in recent history.

Variations on a Theme

As a starting point for analyzing how gold and bitcoin influence a traditional investment portfolio, TMC Research ran a backtest using ETFs representing US stocks, US bonds, gold and bitcoin (see fund list below). The benchmark is a simple 60% equities/40% bonds mix. Variations of this 60/40 mix are tested by incrementally adding gold, bitcoin, or both, with commensurate declines in stocks and bonds.

For example, in the table below, three adjustments to the baseline 60/40 portfolio (grey row at top) are made by adding gold and reducing weights in stocks and bonds (rows highlighted in yellow). As one illustration, the 55% Stocks/35% Bonds/10% Gold (GLD) portfolio (identified as the 55.35.10.gld strategy): 1) cuts stocks by 5 percentage points to a 55% weight; 2) trims bonds from 40% to 35% and; 3) redeploys to gold via a 10% weight. In all, there are three portfolios with varying degrees of a gold allocation at 5%, 10%, and 20%.

The same adjustments to the 60/40 benchmark are applied using bitcoin – for example, the 55.35.10.btc strategy holds a 10% weight in bitcoin, financed by a reduction in stocks and bonds by 5 percentage points each. (The three strategy variations that add bitcoin are highlighted in blue.)

For the third set of strategy tests, portfolios hold equal weights in gold and bitcoin and lower amounts of stocks and bonds (green rows). For instance, one variation of the 60/40 portfolio is revised so that stocks and bonds are cut to 55% and 35% weights, respectively, with 5% shifting to gold and 5% to bitcoin (55.35.5.5 strategy).

Unsurprisingly, the results show that the more gold and/or bitcoin held, the higher the portfolio performance. The caveat is that the sample period tested only dates to 2014, a limitation related to bitcoin’s short history. The years since then primarily reflect a strong bull market for the asset. The original cryptocurrency was launched in 2009, but the data set we’re using (Yahoo Finance) begins in 2014, arguably the year when bitcoin started moving from the fringes of finance into the mainstream. As such, 2014 is the earliest common date for all the assets.

In other words, the bitcoin results reflect a strong bullish bias in the extreme. Simply put, there’s a reasonable case to make that the test presented doesn’t fully capture the risk of a bear cycle that could weigh on the cryptocurrency for an extended period in the future. The same caveat applies also applies to stocks, bonds, and gold, although the scale of bitcoin’s rally is far greater.

Risk Lessons

All that aside, the test highlights several takeaways beyond the fact that recent history has been kind to investors adding gold and/or bitcoin. The first observation: Despite a roaring bull market in alternative assets in recent years, adding bitcoin substantially raises overall portfolio risk.

One could argue the sharply higher risk is worthwhile to earn a significantly higher return. Agreement or disagreement on that point depends on each investor’s objective, time horizon, and other factors that influence how to customize a portfolio for a specific individual (or institution).

The fact remains that history (at least since 2014) shows that holding more bitcoin raises portfolio risk. How much more? It depends on the risk metric. Take maximum drawdown, which measures the deepest peak-to-trough decline. In the table above, the 60/40’s steepest drawdown is -21% -- a non-trivial interim loss that doubles with a 10% weight in bitcoin (55.35.10.btc).

Compare that with a 10% weight in gold, which lowers the maximum drawdown, albeit only slightly, based on the 55.35.10.gld strategy, while lifting performance a bit for the full-sample period.

Crypto advocates can rightly counter that the full-sample return for the strategies that hold bitcoin rise several fold over the 60/40 mix and relative to the strategies holding gold.

A related question: How do the risk-adjusted returns compare? Two metrics are shown to estimate answers: Sharpe ratio and Sortino ratio. Each measures return per unit of risk (return volatility), with the Sharpe ratio treating upside and downside vol equally, while Sortino focuses on downside risk, offering a better approximation of how investors treat real-world volatility. In both cases, a higher reading equates with a higher risk-adjusted performance.

Both risk measures indicate that gold and bitcoin improve risk-adjusted performance. That said, the improvement stalls for allocations above a 10% weight in gold and/or bitcoin.

Overall, the results suggest that while bitcoin is promoted by some as a digital version of gold, the risk profile of the two assets is quite different, at least over the past decade or so. Bitcoin, in short, is far riskier than gold, although so far that higher risk has translated into sharply higher returns for the cryptocurrency.

The crucial question centers on whether the substantially higher performance for bitcoin vs. gold, stocks or bonds is worth the sharp increase in risk? Comparison of the Sharpe ratios suggests not, although there’s more scope for a cautious “yes” based on the Sortino ratios.

Perhaps the bigger hurdle is the maximum-drawdown track record. Everyone wants higher returns, but deciding if the potential for a sharply deeper, if temporary, portfolio loss may be a price too high to bear for some investors.

For most investors, the bitcoin risk hurdle may be a bridge too far in search of portfolio diversification. One reason: digital assets, including bitcoin, are often subject to periods of heightened volatility, commonly referred to as “crypto winters,” which can occur when regulatory developments, macroeconomic conditions, or shifts in risk sentiment are unfavorable. These periods can result in swift and sharp drawdowns, as was the case in much of 2022 and in December of 2017. On the other hand, the results above suggest that a small amount of bitcoin may be sufficiently potent to reap most of the rewards from a strategic portfolio perspective. Gold, by contrast, seems to require a higher weight for relatively meaningful results.

Keep in mind that the rosy view in the current rear-view mirror presented above may be misleading. All the more so when you consider that the next decade for the leading cryptocurrency — with a minuscule (and therefore highly speculative) track record vs. stocks, bonds and gold — could look very different from the previous ten years.

By contrast, stocks, bonds and gold have long histories across many business cycles, which is valuable for gaming out future scenarios. Bitcoin, by contrast, is still in the early innings for proving what it’s capable of, or not, in different macroeconomic conditions.

The backtest above was conducted with the following ETF proxies:

  • * US stocks: SPDR S&P 500 (SPY)

  • * US bonds: iShares Core US Aggregate Bond (AGG)

  • * Gold: SPDR Gold Trust (GLD)

  • Bitcoin: Grayscale Bitcoin Trust (GBTC), backfilled with bitcoin price via Yahoo Finance prior to 2015

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Oxford Industries Continues To Struggle But Trades At More Than 15x Earnings
data.description:
Oxford Industries, Inc. (NYSE:OXM) reported Q2 '25 results, with sales trends worsening from Q1 '25 in a much more important quarter (seasonally). The company commented on a potential improvement in sales into Q3, but with margins down big.

In this context, the company has added debt to pay dividends and buy back shares. I do not agree with this capital allocation policy, given the uncertain context, where net cash (or lower net debt) plays a strategic role.

Based on company guidance, the name trades at 15/17x FY25 earnings. I believe this valuation is very high considering the lack of growth, margin deleverage, risky capital allocation, and the economic context. I maintain a Hold on OXM.

Q2 '25 results

Worsening in a key quarter: Q1 and Q2 are the company's key quarters, in terms of seasonality, given the warm-weather skew of the brands. Of course, Q4 and the Holidays help at the end of the year, but H1 '25 is more important than H2 '25.

In this context, the company's sales worsened in Q2, with sales down 4% versus 1% in Q1. Retail comps were down 5%, same as last quarter, but this time wholesale moved down 6%, versus a positive 4% last quarter.

Brand trend unchanged: By brand, the situation is unchanged from Q1 '25, and one could even say from FY24.

Lilly Pulitzer is the strongest performer, maintaining a small positive LSD figure. Still, this is decelerating from double-digit growth in Q1.

Tommy Bahama is the biggest driver of underperformance because it is the largest brand. It was down big, HSD in comps. This is probably the worst point in the results.

Johnny Was continues to underperform, with low double-digit declines. This is a trend since the brand was acquired, and during the call, management said that the brand is under turnaround efforts with big changes in many areas. JW currently serves as a very worrying sign of bad brand management, as it has never surpassed the sales from the time when the brand was acquired.

Finally, the Emerging Brands segment (which includes about four brands) was up, although I do not understand if there are comparable figures there because of the acquisition of Rogers.

Margins collapsed: The most negative results were not in the topline, but at the bottom line. Margins moved down significantly, losing 50% of operating income YoY ($28 million vs $57 million) and with margins halved (7% vs. 13.5%).

This has been a double whammy between more promotionality (needed to attract traffic in the current economy, and because of assortment and brand problems), and higher CoGS from tariffs (these two leading to 160bps of gross margin declines), and the fact that sales were down despite adding a lot of stores (almost 70 net new stores in FY24 and FY25), meaning SG&A grew in dollars and that it deleveraged 500bps.

Forward may not be better

The company provided one very positive point looking ahead. It guided sales to be LSD down to flat at the aggregate level in Q3, but with stores comping positively, including TB flat.

However, a few things are to be noted. First, last year Q3 was down big, almost 6%, with HSD negative comps in brick and mortar retail, because of hurricane season and the U.S. election. So it is comping an easy quarter. Second, the quarter is the smallest seasonally by almost a 25% versus any other quarter in the year. That means, lower profitability in general from leverage. And finally, and most importantly, margins are expected to remain very challenged, down 300bps on gross margins, and with SG&A up MSD (meaning deleverage versus flat or down LSD aggregate sales).

That is, the only positive development will be the positive comps, with the rest pointing towards a relatively bad Q3.

For Q4, the situation is in flux, with the impact of tariffs (aggregate $25/35 million net of supplier discounts and unmitigated costs) hitting mostly in H2 '25, and with a response from the company that will greatly depend on the demand situation, which, as we have seen, is not great at the moment.

The company maintained guidance in general, with revenues down a little, but margins and EPS flat.

Risky capital allocation

In a context where margins are down big, where profitability at the operating level is much lower, and where important brands are down HSD, I would expect the company to act conservatively in terms of the balance sheet and capital allocation.

This is not what OXM has done this year. YTD, the company has paid dividends of $21 million and repurchased shares for $55 million. Although most of this can be covered with the operating profitability of H1 '25 alone ($66 million in operating profits, so about $50 million in NOPAT), the strategy still led to an increase in debt, to $81 million versus $31 million in Q4 '24. That is, the company has reduced equity by increasing debt.

That is not a conservative way of managing the balance sheet. If the dividend was not expected to be cut, then the company could have saved on the repurchases. We should remember that in the same year, the company has increased CAPEX substantially (to $55 million in H1 '25 and expected to net $120 million in FY25), so that its cash requirements were already high.

Of course, OXM is not currently in a bad financial position, given that $80 million in debt is less than a year of profitability. But still, I believe the strategy is not what I would prefer.

Valuation remains exaggerated

I have maintained a negative view on OXM's valuation since March 2024 (stock down 50%+ since). The correction in the price seems to have come about via the decrease in expected earnings ($2.8/3.2 EPS in guidance for FY25 vs $6.7 in FY24), but not in the multiple associated with those earnings.

The company has some good brands, with pricing power, but still, it has significant top-line and margin challenges, and is not executing well. In addition, the context is fairly negative, both because of tariffs directly impacting margins, and because of consumers becoming more cautious with spending.

Today, OXM trades at a price of $47 per share, or about 15/17x FY25 guided earnings. I do not believe that a company posting comps down 5%, where operating margins are halving, and guiding for even more challenges, deserves to trade at such valuations.

In my opinion, OXM would start to be attractive at half that multiple, meaning either one believes earnings will double soon, or the price has to be much lower.

For that reason, I maintain a Hold rating on OXM.

data.title: Starbucks Doesn't Excite Me Like It Used To (Downgrade)
data.description:
In March 2024, I wrote a bullish article regarding Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX), the massive global coffee chain that millions of consumers across the globe line up for every day. Even though I'm not the biggest fan of it from a consumer perspective, I do like their hot chocolate. And, back in early last year, I found myself bullish about the name because of the expectation of continued growth and how the stock was valued. Since then, however, we have seen some weakness. Even though revenue continues to expand, this is really only because of an increase in location count and the firm's decision to continue raising prices for its customers. At the same time, profitability has been weakening as of late. And as a result, shares have gotten more expensive.

Management is working on making improvements here. But with how the stock has become valued on a forward basis, the picture is not all that exciting to me at the moment. If the firm can return to the level of profitability that it saw even last year, I would be more optimistic. But with significant issues weighing on it and the way the stock is valued, I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" makes the most sense.

A tough time for Starbucks

If you were to look at fundamental data covering just the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years, things should actually look relatively decent for Starbucks. During this time, for instance, revenue for the company expanded from $32.25 billion to $36.18 billion. For much of the company's life, it has benefited from a rise in comparable store sales. This has historically been fueled by rising transactions and higher prices per transaction. Sure enough, in 2022, comparable sales jumped 8%, with the average ticket price growing 5% while the number of transactions grew 3%. In 2023, we saw another 8% rise, fueled by a 3% increase in the number of transactions and a 5% rise in tickets. But then, in 2024, the picture changed. During that year, comparable sales actually dropped by 2%. Even though management said that the firm saw a 2% benefit from higher average tickets, transactions dropped by 4%. It appears as though this weakness can be chalked up to a combination of macroeconomic worries and, to some extent, competitive pressures.

Even though there has been some weakness when it comes to comparable sales, it is important to keep in mind that this is only one piece of the pie. And it is the piece that applies to the company-operated locations that the firm has. Over the last few years, company-operated stores grew from 18,253 to 21,018. But on top of this, the company has also seen growth in the number of stores that are licensed, with that number expanding from 17,458 to 19,181. In total, this means growth in the number of locations and operations from 35,711 to 40,199. For such a large business with a global reach, this is impressive.

As comparable sales started declining, the company's profitability hit a snag. After seeing net income jump from $3.28 billion in 2022 to $4.12 billion in 2023, there was a drop to $3.76 billion last year. Most of this weakness came from its international operations, with a decline in revenue from $7.49 billion to $7.34 billion, pushing operating profits down from $1.23 billion to $1.06 billion. A lot of this pain was the result of a rise in store operating expenses from $2.76 billion to $2.82 billion. This weighed significantly on the bottom line for its international operations. And according to management, the overall decline in profits for that portion of the company was attributable to higher promotional activity, higher wages, and higher benefits. It is disappointing that even higher promotional activities did not succeed in boosting sales and that they had a negative impact on the bottom line of such a significant magnitude.

There were other areas where the company saw weakness as well. Its Channel Development unit, for instance, saw revenue contract from $1.89 billion to $1.77 billion. This was instrumental in pushing operating profits down from $967.6 million to $925.9 million. It does appear, however, that this bottom-line decline was because of a $91.3 million gain on the sale of its Seattle’s Best Coffee brand that the company completed in 2023. Without this, operating profits here would have actually risen. When it comes to its North American operations, meanwhile, revenue grew, but profits still fell, contracting from $5.50 billion to $5.36 billion. Higher investments in employee wages and benefits, the cost of deleveraging caused by weaker comparable sales, and higher promotional activities all worked together to cause this.

Other profitability metrics for the company showed mixed results from 2023 to 2024. Operating cash flow, for instance, grew from $6.01 billion to $6.10 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a virtual flat line at $7.14 billion. On the other hand, EBITDA for the business fell from $6.98 billion to $6.72 billion.

This year, things are looking even more challenging. For the first nine months of 2025, revenue came in at $27.62 billion. That represents an increase of 1.9% compared to the $27.10 billion the company reported the previous year. This growth came even though comparable sales dropped 2%, with a 4% decline in transactions more than offsetting a 2% rise in tickets. Yes, the number of locations in operation grew from 39,477 in the first half of last year to 41,097 at the same time this year. So that certainly helped.

Unfortunately, bottom-line results for the company have proven to be challenging. Net income has plummeted from $2.85 billion to $1.72 billion. Some of this was because of $137 million that the company spent on restructuring initiatives. But most of it involved higher costs, including a rise in store operating expenses from 42.1% of sales to 46.1%. Higher labor expenses, deleveraging associated with the decline in comparable sales, and higher marketing activities caused this pain. Unfortunately, all other profitability metrics for the company followed suit. Operating cash flow plummeted from $4.56 billion to $3.37 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $5.48 billion to $4.59 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company contracted from $5.10 billion to $3.95 billion.

The good news is that management is making changes to improve operations in the long run. This includes uplifting at least 1,000 stores across North America by the end of next year. It also includes the introduction of a new store prototype that will cost 30% less to build while simultaneously sunsetting its mobile order and pickup-only concepts next year. While the last of these items might seem odd, this should not be confused with the company getting rid of mobile orders and pickup-only orders. Rather, it is centered around the fewer than 100 locations that the firm has in operation, many of which are in office buildings and have little or no seating, that the firm will be getting rid of. This is because the firm wants to return to its roots of its coffee house identity. And, most likely, these activities are lower-margin in nature.

Management is also working on launching new menu innovations for next year. Examples that they have already shared with investors include protein cold foam, a new 1971 dark roast coffee, and more. Simultaneously, the company is also supposedly evaluating options regarding its operations in China. Some rumors suggest that it could sell that part of the business for $5 billion or more.

The company's plans also include its recently launched initiative called Green Apron Service, which came out in August of this year. This, according to the company, will involve improving customer service through how the company greets customers, offering customers glasses or mugs when appropriate, putting personalized messages on cups, and other things aimed at improving the feel of going there. Investing more in employees, changing their queue technology to fulfill cafe orders in under 4 minutes on a consistent basis, and more are all examples of this. This follows a broader transformation plan launched by the company in September of last year that management has called Back to Starbucks and that includes some of the items I've discussed already, as well as simplifying its menu by cutting around 30% of items on it, reintroducing the self-serve condiment bars and free refills, and cutting a large number of corporate roles to improve operating costs.

These are all positive things. But until we see some real progress on this front, I think it is too early to become terribly optimistic. If we project our results based on what we have seen so far for this year, the company should generate net profits during 2025 of $2.27 billion. Adjusted operating cash flow should be about $5.99 billion, while EBITDA should come in at around $5.21 billion. In the chart above, we can see how the company is valued using these estimates and using historical results from last year. These numbers aren't bad, but they aren't great. And for a firm that is dealing with some fundamental issues, I think it puts the company more in the fair value range than it does in the undervalued range.

Takeaway

Starbucks is going through an interesting time. I suppose that there is the potential for upside if things go right. But between the troubles the company has, how early it is in its turnaround process, and how the stock is valued, I just don't see a reason to get terribly optimistic. The company looks to be more or less fairly valued at this moment. And because of this, and despite the changes that management has made and will continue to make, I believe downgrading it to a "Hold" makes the most sense.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Energy Transfer, Vertical Aerospace, QuantumScape And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today we're looking at articles published between Sep. 5 and Sep. 11.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Energy Transfer: Massive Distribution Yield And Discount

Dair Sansyzbayev | Strong Buy

My previous bullish thesis about Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET) did not age quite well because its -1.6% total return since May 28 is way worse compared to the broader equity market's. Nevertheless, I remain bullish and reiterate my "Strong Buy rating both for ET and for the company's Series I Fixed Rate Perpetual Preferred Units (NYSE:ET.PR.I) because its fundamentals remain ironclad. Financial performance is improving from the profitability perspective despite flat revenues, which is crucial to maintain ET's compelling distribution yields. ET is poised to benefit from the ongoing AI revolution as well. Last but not least, the stock is still extremely cheap.

Vertical Aerospace: Now An EVTOL Leader (Rating Double Upgrade)

Stephen Tobin | Buy

In November 2022, I first covered Vertical Aerospace (NYSE:EVTL), rating it a Sell. It was the first Sell article to be published on Seeking Alpha, and since then, 11 articles have been published, with 10 rating it a Sell or Strong Sell and one a Hold.

Today, I am moving to a bullish stance, a clearly contrarian viewpoint, and with the stock down 89% since my first article, I am running against the trend, being greedy when others are fearful, as the saying goes.

QuantumScape: Huge Progress Overlooked (Rating Upgrade)

Stone Fox Capital | Buy

Despite a lot of positive developments, QuantumScape Corporation (NYSE:QS) is actually trading much lower than it was a couple of months ago. The first live demonstration of an electric vehicle powered by QuantumScape solid-state batteries was a huge step forward towards a production launch. My investment thesis is more Bullish on the stock after the positive development of the last few months, combined with QuantumScape slipping back down to $8 for a much better technical setup.

Bristol-Myers Squibb: Growth Portfolio Surpasses Legacy, Driving Oncology And Immunology Leadership

The J Thesis | Strong Buy

Bristol-Myers Squibb (NYSE:BMY) is a global biopharmaceutical heavyweight company that has significantly underperformed the broader market over the past 5 years, with its stock price declining 23%.

The company is discovering and developing innovative medicines for a wide range of diseases in therapeutic areas like oncology, hematology, and cardiovascular conditions. With innovative focus on immuno-oncology, the company is shifting away from a declining legacy business and pivoting to a fast-growing pipeline which is designed to offset patent expiries of legacy products.

BMY trades at a forward P/E of 7.26, which is nearly 60% lower than the industry average of 18.09, indicating limited downside and potential for a rebound. Despite the revenue setback from legacy blockbusters, the company's growth portfolio sales increased 17% year-over-year, suggesting a turnaround may be near. I think the company presents an attractive opportunity for long-term investors while it trades at such undervalued levels.

That is why I think Bristol-Myers Squibb is a Strong Buy with a $54 price target over the next 12 months, indicating nearly 13% upside — and over 18% when taking into account the dividend yield of 5.23%. The company's legacy business is in decline, which justifies a major correction in its stock price, but I think it may be positioned for a long-term turnaround.

CommScope: Amphenol Deal Offers Fresh Start, But Questions Remain

Ian Bezek | Hold

I've been following electronic components manufacturer Amphenol (APH) for quite a few years now. I've always been impressed with that management team's ability to produce strong growth and elevated profitability out of what might, on the face of it, seem like a fairly mundane industry. And now, with the AI and data center-led boom, Amphenol has delivered absolutely sizzling results over the past couple of years.

So it was with interest that I noted Amphenol's latest acquisition target, CommScope's (NASDAQ:COMM) broadband connectivity and cable unit ("CCS"). On August 8th, Amphenol announced that it will be paying $10.5 billion for CCS. On August 22nd, Amphenol secured $4 billion in loan facilities, which should help enable this deal to close promptly.

This is an absolutely massive deal for CommScope. At one point earlier this year, CommScope had a market capitalization of less than $1 billion. And even after the rally post-Amphenol deal announcement, shares are still quoted at around a $3.5 billion market capitalization today. With Amphenol paying $10.5 billion for CommScope's largest business unit, does that leave more upside for COMM stock going forward?

Synopsys: Selloff Brings Shares Down, But Not Down Enough

Grassroots Trading | Hold

I last rated Synopsys, Inc. (NASDAQ:SNPS)a Hold. Valuation did most of the heavy lifting on that call with shares priced as if the company already had all its initiatives in the bag. This was while new leverage for the pending Ansys deal and policy risk around China still loomed. Yes, the China sales clearance improved the setup. Even so, at the time, I felt the multiple had already captured that good news and then some.

Since my rating, and prior to this morning’s gruesome reaction to its earnings (shares trading down around 23%) SNPS did manage to have a better pace than the broader market, rising about 11% versus around 4% for the S&P 500 (SPY).

My view is that demand for electronic design automation is strong and the Ansys deal should give Synopsys a helpful hand. But that upside is balanced out by a stock that’s still priced high, more debt on the books, and risks tied to China and chip foundries.

SPYT: 20% Yields And Limits Of Option "Income"

Trapping Value | Strong Sell

Bull markets can make you feel invulnerable. Tariffs? Recession? Highest valuations in history? No problem. This train has only one direction. Unfortunately, bull markets cannot bend the laws of math (though they can temporarily fool the law of gravity). The law in question here is that the higher the current valuation gets, the worse it will get for future retirees. We will get more into that in another article, but for now, let's look at a fund that is using the current market madness to advertise like this.
